name: East Timorese Portuguese

ancestors.notes: European Portuguese colonial base. East Timor (then Portuguese Timor) was a Portuguese colony from 1702 until the Indonesian invasion in 1975; Portuguese was restored as a co-official language after independence in 2002.

Tetum (Tetun) adstrate/substrate: the national lingua franca, co-official with Portuguese, spoken by >80% of the population (Albuquerque 2010:272). Tetum is Austronesian and, like the other native Timoric languages (Mambae, Galolen, Tokodede), does NOT reduce (lenite) unstressed vowels (Albuquerque 2010:275, fn.7); this substrate trait is the single most consistent phonological feature transferred into East Timorese Portuguese.

Indonesian (bahasa indonesia) adstrate: 24 years of occupation (1975–1999), during which Portuguese was banned, left influence especially on speakers schooled under the occupation (Albuquerque 2010:271, 274).

notes: East Timorese Portuguese / Português de Timor Leste (PTL). Co-official with Tetum; Portuguese is an L2 for nearly all speakers (the 2001 Human Development Report cited by Albuquerque 2010:272 records only ~5% fluent). This spec models the OFFICIAL/ACROLECTAL variety used in formal domains (law, university, church), the norm-oriented end of the continuum Albuquerque (2010:273, fig.1) draws between 'norma europeia' (urban Dili/Baucau, highly schooled speakers) and 'português acrioulado' (rural, L1-transfer-heavy). It is NOT a creole: the Portuguese-based creoles of the region — the now-moribund Bidau Creole Portuguese of Dili (Baxter 1990) and the neighbouring Macau/Malacca creoles — are separate contact languages modelled elsewhere. MODELLED FEATURES (all Albuquerque 2010): (1) NO UNSTRESSED VOWEL REDUCTION — the Tetum substrate keeps full vowels, so EP's schwa/[ɨ] reduction does not apply: bate [ˈba.te], roda [ˈɾɔ.da], enredo [enˈe.dɔ] (p.275, fn.7). This spec therefore OVERRIDES the inherited pt-PT positional vowel reduction, mapping unstressed a→[a], e→[e], o→[o]. (2) ALVEOLAR RHOTIC [r]/[ɾ]; the EP uvular [ʁ] is absent (p.278, e.g. perguntar [peˈgun.ta]). (3) DIGRAPH SIMPLIFICATION: ⟨lh⟩→[l] and ⟨nh⟩→[n] are frequent (olho [ˈo.liu], vinho [ˈbi.niu], p.276); the palatal [ʎ]/[ɲ] realisations survive only near the European norm, so both are listed with the simplified variant first. (4) ALVEOLAR CODA /s/ (no obligatory Lisbon chiado): escola [isˈkɔ.la] ~ [iʃˈkɔ.la] varies (p.277); the alveolar variant is taken as the norm and the inherited PT_CODA_S/Z_HUSH rules are overridden by id. (5) PAROXYTONE STRESS TENDENCY: primary stress falls predominantly on the penult, even where EP differs, with near-absence of proparoxytones — an Austronesian typological transfer (p.279–280; Zanten & Goedemans on fixed penultimate stress in ~2/3 of Austronesian languages). VARIABLE / BASILECTAL, NOT MODELLED (documented in Albuquerque but confined to the acrioulado end and too variable to encode as the acrolectal norm): consonant substitutions ʃ→s, ʒ→z, v→b, f→p and vowel raising e→i / o→u (p.276–277); denasalisation of nasal diphthongs, e.g. educação [e.du.kaˈsa.u] (p.278); final-consonant apocope, esp. in infinitives, e.g. abraçar [aˈba.sa], cair [kai] (p.278–279) — a deletion the engine's allophone layer does not express, left as a documented engine limit; metathesis/epenthesis (advogado [a.di.boˈga.do], p.278); and the H–L% phrasal intonation from the Timoric sprachbund (p.281), which is prosodic and out of base scope. TIER: research — grounded in one fully-read primary phonological/prosodic source (Albuquerque 2010) plus the EP inventory baseline; no gold benchmark exists for this variety.

sources.author: Albuquerque, Davi Borges de
sources.title: Peculiaridades prosódicas do português falado em Timor Leste
sources.notes: PRIMARY, read in full. Section 2.1 (pp.276–277) phoneme realisations; 2.2 (pp.277–279) phonological processes; 2.3 (pp.279–281) prosody; fn.7 p.275 documents the no-vowel-reduction Tetum substrate trait that this spec models.

sources.author: Mateus, M. H. & d'Andrade, E.
sources.title: The Phonology of Portuguese
sources.notes: European Portuguese phoneme inventory used as the baseline against which PTL is described; quoted via Albuquerque (2010:276), not read directly here.

sources.author: Hull, Geoffrey
sources.title: A Morphological Overview of the Timoric Sprachbund
sources.publisher: Studies in Language and Culture of East Timor, v. 4, pp. 98–205
sources.notes: Establishes Timor as a linguistic area (sprachbund); source for the Austronesian substrate context, cited via Albuquerque (2010:281).

sources.author: Baxter, Alan N.
sources.title: Notes on the Creole Portuguese of Bidau, Timor
sources.publisher: Journal of Pidgin and Creole Languages, v. 5, n. 1, pp. 1–38
sources.notes: Describes the Bidau Creole Portuguese of Dili — a SEPARATE Portuguese-based contact language, not the L2 Portuguese modelled here; included to mark the creole-vs-Portuguese distinction.

sources.author: Thomaz, Luís Filipe F. R.
sources.title: Babel Loro Sa'e: o problema linguístico de Timor Leste
sources.notes: Socio-historical background on the Timorese language situation; cited via Albuquerque (2010).

stress.notes: Acordo Ortográfico 1990 conventions inherited from pt-PT: paroxytone default; written accents override; final -i/-u/-r/-l/-z/-x/-im/-om/-um/-ns and nasal diphthongs attract final stress. Albuquerque (2010:279–280) documents that PTL reinforces the paroxytone default — speakers regularise even EP-irregular stress toward the penult and almost never produce proparoxytones — under Austronesian influence; this is a tendency the orthography-predictable rules already approximate, not an extra rule set. DIPHTHONGS: declared so the bundled syllabifier counts nuclei correctly — without them every vowel run merges into one nucleus and co-e-lho is read as coe-lho, one syllable short, putting the stress and its vowel reduction on the wrong vowel. ⟨oe⟩ is hiatus, not a diphthong (Cunha & Cintra, §Ditongos).

allophone_rules.notes: East Timorese Portuguese keeps an alveolar coda /s/ as the acrolectal norm (no obligatory Lisbon 'chiado'); the palatal [ʃ] surfaces only variably near the European norm (Albuquerque 2010:277, escola [isˈkɔla] ~ [iʃˈkɔla]). This overrides the inherited pt-PT PT_CODA_S_HUSH rule by id, matching the alveolar coda in this spec's positional_graphemes.
Voiced counterpart of PT_CODA_S_HUSH: alveolar coda /z/ retained; overrides the inherited pt-PT PT_CODA_Z_HUSH rule by id.

location.notes: Dili. Portuguese is an L2 for nearly all speakers: the capital stands in for the country. [Wikidata Q9310 P625]
